coredata.dc:title: Reaction-time-controlled carbon dots nanoarchitectonics with stable and bright photoluminescence for white light emission
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Xuanqi
Yang, Qiaoyu
Liu, Chenchen
Yang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: It is still a challenge for multicolor carbon dots (CDs) with stable and bright photoluminescence (PL). The controlling of synthesis becomes a hot topic. In this paper, CDs with blue-, green- and red-emitting named as B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were prepared using three isomers of phenylenediamine as precursors by one-step solvothermal synthesis. Green and red emitting CDs with C, N and O components named as samples G-CDs and R-CDs revealed uniform nanoparticle distribution with average sizes of 7.2 and 5.7 nm, respectively. The PL peak wavelength of three kinds of CDs were 430, 546 and 613 nm, and their average fluorescence lifetime was 5.32, 22.35, 8.74 ns, respectively. The full width at half maximum of PL spectra of the samples was 65.12, 92.63, 83.62 nm, and their PL quantum yields were 8.19, 43.43, and 5.14%, respectively. The brightly luminescent CDs were further embedded in prepared polyvinyl alcohol films to prepare a white emitting layer by adjusting the ratios of CDs with different emitting color. The composite CDs revealed stable and bright white emitting in the film with a Commission Internationale d'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of (0.28, 0.33). The result suggested that these unique luminescent CDs have potential applications in various luminescence fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
White emitting
Luminescent film

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for preparation of CDs.
(a, b) TEM images of sample G-CDs. (c to e) TEM images of sample R-CDs. (f) Size distribution of sample R-CDs. The particle number evaluating for the size distribution was 70.
XPS spectra of CDs samples. (a - c), C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of B-CDs. (d -f), C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of G-CDs. (g - i), C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of R-CDs.
(a) XRD patterns of samples G-CDs and R-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectra of samples B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of CDs samples. PL spectra of samples (b) B-CDs, (c) G-CDs, and (d) R-CDs using different excited wavelength. (e) Fluorescent decay plots of samples CDs. (f) PL spectra of CDs samples compared with CDs film samples. The inset in (a) shows the pictures of CDs samples under room light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) irradiation.
(a) Preparation illustration of luminescent CDs films. (b) Pictures of the as-prepared CDs film samples under room light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) irradiation.
(a) PL spectrum of W-CDs. (b) CIE coordinates of samples B-, G-, R-, and W-CDs. The inset in (b) shows the picture of a W-CDs film under 365 nm UV light irradiation.
Atomic ratio of element C, N and O and O/C molar ratio for samples B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs.
PL properties of sample CDs and CDs films.
Components B1 and B2, time constants τ1 and τ2, as well as average lifetime τaverage, of sample B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Reaction-time-controlled carbon dots nanoarchitectonics with stable and bright photoluminescence for white light emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International School of Nanshan Shenzhen, 518000, Shenzhen, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International School of Nanshan Shenzhen, 518000, Shenzhen, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is still a challenge for multicolor carbon dots (CDs) with stable and bright photoluminescence (PL). The controlling of synthesis becomes a hot topic. In this paper, CDs with blue-, green- and red-emitting named as B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were prepared using three isomers of phenylenediamine as precursors by one-step solvothermal synthesis. Green and red emitting CDs with C, N and O components named as samples G-CDs and R-CDs revealed uniform nanoparticle distribution with average sizes of 7.2 and 5.7 nm, respectively. The PL peak wavelength of three kinds of CDs were 430, 546 and 613 nm, and their average fluorescence lifetime was 5.32, 22.35, 8.74 ns, respectively. The full width at half maximum of PL spectra of the samples was 65.12, 92.63, 83.62 nm, and their PL quantum yields were 8.19, 43.43, and 5.14%, respectively. The brightly luminescent CDs were further embedded in prepared polyvinyl alcohol films to prepare a white emitting layer by adjusting the ratios of CDs with different emitting color. The composite CDs revealed stable and bright white emitting in the film with a Commission Internationale d'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of (0.28, 0.33). The result suggested that these unique luminescent CDs have potential applications in various luminescence fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), has obtained extensive concerned from researchers owing to its excellent optical properties, spectral tunability, wide and easy availability of raw materials, environmental friendliness and high stability, these materials have great advantages [1–3]. It is widely used in light-emitting diodes [4–8], light-emitting films [9,10], biological imaging [11–13], photoelectric conversion [14,15], information encryption and storage [15,16] and other fields. The synthesis control of CDs is still a hot topic. A bottom-up solvothermal method was normally used to synthesize CDs because this method was relatively simple and easy to control. During the preparation process, CDs with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) were created by one step in the reactor without further oxidation and passivation.

Multi-color CDs are increasingly used in the study of light-emitting devices because of their outstanding optical properties and chemical inertness. Nowadays, many reports in literature have prepared light-emitting diodes (LED) by preparing CDs or embedding CDs into polymer films [17,18]. For example, Wang et al. successfully developed red-emitting CDs with PLQYs of up to 53%, which were successfully applied to white LEDs, achieving color rendering index (CRI) of 97.4 Xu et al. successfully prepared blue-, green- and pink-emitting CDs using three isomers of phenylenediamine as precursors and the CDs were used to prepare luminescent films [19]. Ding et al. produced four-color emitting CDs with tunable fluorescence wavelength using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as raw materials by solvent-controlled method. The luminescent composite films of multi-color CDs were successfully prepared and the white-light-emitting polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film was prepared by adjusting the ratio of multi-color CDs [7]. These prepared LEDs or photoluminescence (PL) conversion layers revealed excellent PL properties, suggesting important practical application in various emitting yields.

Although CDs have revealed a series of great properties, they are certain drawbacks, such as wide PL spectra and low PLQYs. The synthesis of CDs with narrow PL spectra still faced many difficulties. There are many reasons for this difference in the luminescence of CDs, which may be due to the different preparation methods of CDs, which cause CDs have different optical properties [20–24]. Many elements influence the luminescent properties of CDs, such as the content of graphite-N and dehydration and carbonization degree of CDs surface [7,25]. It is very important to explore whether the luminescence of CDs is affected by surface-state-controlling, which is helpful to probe the luminescence principle of CDs. At present, the PL spectra of multicolor CDs prepared in literature have a wide range, and a large the full width at half maximum (FWHM), which proves that the light uniformity of CDs is poor [26]. Wang et al. synthesized CDs (B-CQDs, G-CQDs and R-CQDs) apply solvothermal method, and FWHM of tricolor CDs were 90, 85 and 105 nm, respectively [4]. Zhang et al. using p-phenylenediamine as raw material and adjusting the solvent prepared tricolor emission CDs. The FWHM of PL spectra of samples CDs were 126, 123, 73 nm, respectively [10]. Compared with the result in literature, the tricolor CDs with narrow PL spectra is still desired. Therefore, it is of great practical significance to the PL spectra of CDs with small FWHM, which improved the purity of the emission color of CDs, thereby improving the color saturation of light-emitting devices. At the same time, the PLQYs also affect the application of CDs. Thus, the quantity of fluorescent photons launched by the material after absorbing fluorescence becomes more and decreasing the quantity of photons of the absorbed fluorescence. The improvement of PLQY is of great significance for the luminescence intensity of CDs [27]. Jiang et al. successfully prepared blue-, green- and pink-emitting CDs using three isomers of phenylenediamine, and the PLQYs were 4.8,10.4, and 20.6%, respectively [1]. The PLQYs of samples B-, G- and R-CDs, in our study, were 8.19, 43.43, and 5.14%, respectively, and the PLQY of G-CDs was better than that reported in the literature.

In this paper, an efficient one-step solvothermal synthesis was used to synthesize tricolor CDs with bright PL brightness. Namely, CDs with blue, green, and red emitting were prepared by controlling reaction time using three isomers of phenylenediamine as precursors. The PL peak wavelength range of CDs was 400–650 nm, capping majority visible light region, and had narrow FWHMs. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs is up to 22.35 ns. The PLQY of CDs is as high as 43.43%. Blue, green, red, and white PL layers were produced by mixing CDs with PVA polymer. The prepared PL layers have stable and bright PL properties.

Chemicals. M-phenylenediamine (MPD), O-phenylenediamine (OPD) and P-phenylenediamine (PPD) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethanol was taken from Tianjin Fuyu Chemical Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Mw = 44.05) was obtained from Aladdin. Deionized water used in experiments was from a Millipore system (SYNSV0000, Millipore, France). All chemicals used in experiments were analytically pure and without further purification.

Characterization. The microstructure of CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), which equipment used was JEM-2010 (JEOL, Japan). The element component analysis of CDs was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which apparatus used ESCA 3000 (Thermo Fisher SCIENTIFIC, America). The internal molecular structure of CDs was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), which instrument used Rigaku SmartLab 9 KW with Cu Kα radiation (Rigaku, Japan). The surface group structure of CDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), which equipment used Nicolet 380 (Thermo Electron, America). The scanning range was 500–4000 cm−1. The absorption spectra of samples were measured by UV–vis spectra, which equipment used U-4100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi 4100, Japan), and the scanning range was 200–800 nm. The PL spectra of CDs were recorded using a F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), and the scanning range was 300–800 nm wit slits of 2.5 and 5 nm. The fluorescence decay plots of CDs were analyzed by Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer (Horiba, Japan). A UV lamp used for the stability test of CDs samples was WFH-204B ultraviolet analyzer (Shanghai Chitang Industry, China).

Synthesis of CDs. An efficient method, one-step solvothermal, was used to prepare multicolor luminescent CDs. By controlling the reaction solvent and reaction temperature, adjusting the reaction solute and reaction time, different luminescent tricolor CDs solutions were obtained.

For the synthesis of B-CDs, 0.004 g of MPD was dispersed in 20 mL of ethanol, sonication treatment for 15 min to make the solute evenly dispersed. The mixture was then moved to a 50 mL PTFE-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h.19 A blue luminescent CDs solution was obtained after natural cooling to atmospheric temperature. The sample was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 20 min. A blue CDs solution was further filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to gain a clear blue emitting CDs solution named as B-CDs.

As for the synthesis of G-CDs, typically, 0.01 g of OPD was added to 20 mL ethanol with sonication treatment for 15 min to make the solute evenly dispersed. The mixture was then moved to a 50 mL PTFE-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Green luminescent CDs solution was gained after natural cooling to air temperature. The sample was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 20 min. Next, a green CDs solution was further filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to get a clarified green emitting CDs solution named as G-CDs.

The synthesis of R-CDs: 0.1 g of PPD was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol, sonication treatment for 15 min to make the solute evenly dispersed. The mixture was placed in 50 mL PTFE-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The red luminescent CDs solution was gained after natural cooling to atmospheric temperature. The sample was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 20 min. Then the sample solution was further filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to gain a clarified red fluorescent CDs solution named as R-CDs.

Preparation of luminescent films. Typically, 2 g of PVA (MW = 44.05) was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water with stirring at 60 °C for 1 h. As-prepared CDs solutions were then mixed with PVA solution under heating and stirring conditions. Drop the mixed solution onto a clean glass plate and dry at room temperature for 10 h. After that, the dried CDs film samples was peeled off from the glass substrate to obtain uniform and transparent tricolor CDs film samples. The prepared CDs film samples has excellent flexibility, ductility and photoluminescent properties.

The preparation procedure of CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . M-PD, O-PD, P-PD and ethanol were served as carbon and nitrogen precursors. The solvothermal synthesis temperature of CDs with different emitting color was same and their reaction time was different because of the effect of different precursors with different carbonized degree. A one-step solvothermal method activated the PL properties of phenylenediamine. The PL of CD depended strongly on the surface functional groups. The carbonization of precursors affected the resulting PL properties of CDs due to the volume and surface defects and surface groups. Because CDs samples were prepared in an ethanol solution, the stability of the CDs in polar solvents can become better. In addition, the CDs were benefitted from ethanol which made the CDs with clear surface and easy dispersion.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of green and red emitting CDs samples and the size distribution of red one. In Fig. 2a and b, sample G-CDs revealed an average size of 7.2 ± 2.5 nm (size distribution in Fig. S1, see Supporting Information). The interface among nanoparticles was not clearly. The CDs connected each other (the TEM image in Fig. S1, see Supporting Information). B-CDs revealed similar situation with G-CDs. In contrast, sample R-CDs revealed clear surface and well dispersion in Fig. 2c and d. Fig. 2e shows the TEM image of a particle. Lattice fringes were observed clearly. Sample R-CDs revealed an average size of 5.7 ± 2.5 nm. Sample R-CDs exhibited much narrow size distribution and well dispersion. The HRTEM image of a carbon dot in Fig. 2e revealed that R-CDs revealed clearly lattice fringes with a space of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene [28,29].

The XPS spectra of CDs were analyzed (survey spectra in Fig. S2, see Supporting Information), suggesting that CDs were chiefly composed of C, N and O elements. Fig. 3 shows the high resolution XPS spectra of CDs. The C 1s spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 3a, d, and 3g, in which samples revealed similar C 1s spectra. The peaks of C 1s spectrum of B-CDs sample was at 284.5, 286.3 and 288 eV, those of sample G-CDs was at 284.8, 286.5 and 288.2 eV, and those of R-CDs sample was at 284.7, 286.2 and 288.2 eV, which corresponding to C-C/CC, C-N/C-O and CN/CO bonding, respectively [27]. For the C 1s spectrum, the C-C/CC peak in the samples gradually decreased. Fig. 3b, e, and 3h show the N 1s spectra of CDs, which was fitted into three typical peaks corresponding to pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N bonding, respectively. The center of B-CDs sample was at 398.4, 399 and 400.5 eV, G-CDs sample was at 398.1, 399 and 400.2 eV, and R-CDs sample was at 398.3, 398.9 and.

400.0 eV. In N 1s high-resolution spectra, when the PL peak wavelength of the CDs was gradually red-shifted from 430 to 613 nm, the peaks of pyridinic N and amino N gradually decreased while the peak of Pyrrolic N gradually increased because the presence of pyrrolic N was conducive to the formation of CDs with long-wavelength. The O 1s spectra of CDs (Fig. 3c, f, and 3i) were fitted into two typical peaks belonging to CO and C-O bonding. The peaks of CO and C-O bonding of B-CDs were at 531.8 and 532.4 eV, respectively while those of G-CDs were at 531.9 and 532.9 eV as well as those of R-CDs were at 531.7 and 532.5 eV [26]. XPS spectra confirmed abundant functional groups formed on the surface of the CDs and the slight difference of functional groups affected the PL behavior.

For the purpose to further explore the effect of components on the luminescence of CDs, XPS analysis was finished. The atomic ratios of elements are illustrated in Table 1 (the histogram ratio in Fig. S3, see Supporting Information). The C ratios of samples B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs were 80.43, 82.86, and 70.22%, respectively. The element O ratios of samples were 9.20, 8.84, and 14.83%, respectively. The N element content of samples were 6.95, 7.00, and 7.40%, respectively. The C ratio of R-CDs was significantly lower than those of B-CDs and G-CDs, while the N ratio of R-CDs was obviously high compared with B- CDs and G-CDs. High C ratio and low N ratio resulted that sample R-CDs revealed the highest C/N ratio. The O/C ratios of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs were 0.11, 0.11 and 0.21, respectively, and the ratios gradually increased [10]. The result indicates that the emission wavelengths of CDs may be red-shifted, in which the surface oxidation degree increased progressively from B-CDs to R-CDs.

The CDs were characterized adopt FT-IR and XRD to further understand its structure and functional group components of CDs. According to the XRD patterns in Fig. 4 a, both G-CDs and R-CDs samples had broad peaks at about 24°, which corresponded to the (100) plane spacing of graphite [30]. As shown in Fig. 4b, CDs samples prepared by three isomers of phenylenediamine exhibited similar characteristic functional groups. FTIR spectra were measured out to obtain information on the chemical bonds on CDs surface. CDs had abundant polar functional groups, suggesting that CDs had excellent water solubility. Such as, the stretching vibration bands of CDs appeared at 3350–3550 and 2800∼3000 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H and the sp3 hybridized C-H bond. The bands appeared at 1450∼1750 cm−1 imputed to the stretching vibration of CC and CN bands. The bands at 850∼1050 cm−1 were owed to the extensional vibration of olefin = C-H bond on CDs surface. These results indicate the presence of hydrophilic groups in CDs, suggesting that CDs samples have high water solubility [30–34]. The result supplied a support for CDs-PVA emitting films with homogeneous CDs distribution were created in an aqueous solution as discussed later.

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of samples were characterized to explore their optical performance. Fig. 5 a shows the absorption spectra of samples. Samples B-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs revealed different adsorption spectra and the absorption bands at about 350, 380 and 500 nm was imputed to the n-π* transition corresponding to the difference of CO bonding in various CDs. Fig. 5b-d shows the PL spectra of samples CDs at different excitation wavelength. The excited wavelengths of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were 320–420, 360–480, and 420–580 nm, respectively. The PL spectra of B-CDs changed with different excited wavelength. Different organic functional groups on the surface of CDs resulted in the excitation wavelength dependence. In contrast, the PL peak wavelength of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs almost remain unchanged. The FWHM of PL spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs was 65.12, 92.63 and 83.62 nm, respectively. Three samples revealed single PL peak, which indicates that CDs samples had eximious monochromatic luminescence properties. The results suggest that there may be a certain optical center inside the CDs [18]. Narrow PL spectra is beneficial to improve the fluorescence contrast of CDs and further improve the color saturation of LED.

The PL peak wavelengths of samples B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were at 450, 550 and 610 nm, respectively. It is found that the PL wavelengths of B-CDs revealed a red shift using 400 and 420 nm excitation, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity was also decreased. This is ascribed to the occurrence of Stokes shift, the electrons in the excited state (S1) undergo vibrational relaxation, internal conversion and other ways to transition to the ground state (S0), resulting in energy loss, which is reflected in the spectrum. The maximum excitation wavelength of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were 360, 384 and 493 nm, respectively, and the corresponding optimal emission wavelengths are 429, 546 and 613 nm, respectively as illustrated Table 2 . The fluorescent decay plot of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs are shown in Fig. 5e. The fluorescence decay curves were fitted by a single exponential function. The average fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were 5.32, 22.35 and 8.74 ns, respectively. The fluorescence decay parameters are listed in Table 3 . Sample G-CDs revealed the longest lifetime, which related their surface state because it also exhibited the largest FWHM of PL spectra compared with B-CDs and R-CDs. Furthermore, the different degree of hydrogen bond interaction between CDs and ethanol may also lead the difference of the lifetime.

When the CDs samples were excited at 360, 384 and 493 nm, the corresponding relative PLQYs of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were 8.19, 43.43, and 5.14%, respectively. The PLQYs of G-CD was the highest among the three CDs, indicating that G-CDs had stronger fluorescence intensity. The stability plays a key role for practical applications. As-prepared CDs revealed high stability. The evolution of PL intensity of sample after storing for 220 days at room temperature is shown in Fig. S4 (a-c) (see Supporting Information), which proved CDs with high stability. Although the PL intensity of the CDs revealed a certain decrease, it still revealed bright PL. Compared with B-CDs and R-CDs, the decrease of PL intensity of G-CDs is the smallest, suggesting high stability. CDs samples exhibited high stability against photobleaching. After continuously irradiating for 3 h under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the decrease of PL intensity of the CDs was not too much as shown in Figs. S4d–f (see Supporting Information), suggesting CDs samples. Because of samples B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs revealed high brightness and stability, they were used to prepare flexible PL layers [35–40]. The CDs were embedded PVA films by preparing aqueous CDs solutions. Fig. 6 a shows the preparation process of the CDs films. Aqueous CDs solutions were first mixed with PVA solutions. Because of a transparent solution obtained, the aggregation of CDs did not occur during preparation, suggesting the CDs with high stability against polar solvents. Resulting films were obtained by coating the mixture solution on a slide glass. As-dried films were peeled off from the glass plate with tweezers. As shown in Fig. 6b (left), tricolor CDs films was transparent under room light. The red color of the R-CDs-PVA film is ascribed to the broad adsorption band of the CDs in visible light region (Fig. 5a). After completely drying, the CDs film samples got rid of the influence of water on the PL of CDs, revealing same PL behaviors as in ethanol solvent. The CDs films samples show bright blue, green, and red PL under 365 nm UV light irradiation as shown in Fig. 6b (right). As-prepared CDs films were highly transparent and can be cut into any size and shape as required. Thus, the films as a PL conversion layer can be applied to fabricate LEDs. The CDs film samples has good stability, and long-term placement in air will not affect its photoluminescence properties.

The PL spectra of CDs film samples are shown in Fig. 5f and Fig. S5 (see Supporting Information). In the case of excited wavelengths of 367, 390 and 511 nm, the PL peak wavelengths of blue, green, and red emitting CDs films were 450, 531 and 595 nm, respectively as illustrated in Table 3. The PL excitation wavelengths of the CDs film samples have different degrees of blue-shift or red-shift, which is due to the addition of PVA. The PL peak wavelength of the B-CDs film sample revealed a red shift from 430 to 450 nm, while the PL peaks wavelengths of G-CDs revealed a blue shift from 546 to 531 nm. As for the R-CDs films, a blue shift was observed from 613 to 595 nm. Compared with initial CDs, the width of PL spectra of the films became large for the blue emitting film from 65.12 to 100.81 nm. In contrast, the PL spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs films became narrow, in which the FWHM of PL spectrum the green emitting film was 90.63 nm while that of initial G-CDs was 92.63 nm. Furthermore, the PL spectrum of R-CDs in the film was blue-shifted and the FWHM was decreased into 76.31 from 83.62 nm. The red-shifted and narrowed PL spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs in PVA films are described to the formation of hydrogen bonding between PVA molecules and CDs as well as the dipole polarization in the solution. Thus, the surface state of the CDs changed to change the PL of the CDs. Such narrow and bright PL from the CDs in solid PVA films supplied a possibility to fabricate emitting conversion layers for white LEDs.

To establish a set of technical standards for defining and measuring colors, Commission Internationale d'Eclairage (CIE) developed basic standards and measurement procedures in lighting field. The CIE coordinates of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs prepared were calculated to be (0.16, 0.06), (0.23, 0.44) and (0.48, 0.51), respectively. Using blue, green, and red emitting CDs, white-emitting CDs (W-CDs) solutions were obtained by adjusting the ratios of the CDs with different color. A W-CDs film sample exhibited bright white fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation as shown in Fig. 7 a. The CIE coordinates of sample W-CDs was (0.28, 0.33) as shown in Fig. 7b. It was well known that the CIE coordinates of standard white emission was (0.33, 0.33). Kumari et al. reported the preparation of a white conversion layer with PVA matrix, and the CIE coordinates of the white CDs emission was (0.33,0.45) [41]. An et al. prepared red, green, and blue CDs using OPD as raw material [42]. White CD were prepared by adjusting the ratio of CDs. The CIE coordinates of white CDs in the literature was (0.38, 0.37). By changing the ratios of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, the PL spectra and color coordinates were changed. The emitting color of CDs/PVA films from cold white (CW) 4200 K to warm white (WW) 2850 K can be adjusted accordingly. The PL spectrum of as-prepared CDs covered in whole visible light region from 400 to 700 nm as shown in Fig. 7a.

Highly stable and bright multicolor CDs were created by solvothermal synthesis using three kinds of phenylenediamine in ethanol. The CDs samples prepared by one-step solvothermal method had stable PL properties. Three kinds of emitting CDs revealed blue, green, and red PL peak at 430 (B-CDs), 546 (G-CDs), and 613 nm (R-CDs), respectively. Sample B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs exhibited related narrowed PL spectra and with average fluorescence lifetimes of 5.32, 22.35, 8.74 ns, respectively. These brightly luminescent CDs were further embedded in PVA films to prepare an emitting conversion layer for white LEDs. The composite CDs revealed stable and bright white emitting in the film with a Commission Internationale d'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of (0.28, 0.33). After long-term storge in the air, these films remained their initial PL intensity, indicating that these unique luminescent CDs have potential applications in various luminescent fields. This study can provide a promising method for the preparation of brightly PL CDs. It also suggests broad application for CDs as light-emitting conversion layers.

Xuanqi Hao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Qiaoyu Yang: Writing – review & editing. Chenchen Liu: Formal analysis. Ping Yang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no competing Conflict interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the projects from the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (grant no. 51972145), Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, China (grant no. 2019GXRC016 and 2021GXRC109), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan, China (XKY2118).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111594.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Angew Chem Int Ed

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. Wu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging, angew. chem. Int. Ed. 54 (2015) 5360-5363. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201501193.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural evolution of carbon dots during low temperature pyrolysis
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Luo, L. Lari, H. Kim, S. Herou, L.C. Tanase, V.K. Lazarov, M. Titirici, Structural evolution of carbon dots during low temperature pyrolysis, Nanoscale 14 (2022) 910-918. https://doi.org/10.1039/d1nr07015k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: materials, synthesis, properties and approaches to long-wavelength and multicolor emission
J Mater Chem B
